title: Insert interval
description: Insert a new interval into sorted non-overlapping intervals

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize result = [], inserted = False, newInterval = [2,5]. Step 2: Process interval [1,3]: Check overlap: 3 >= 2 and 1 <= 5 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(2,1)=1, newInterval.end = max(5,3)=5, newInterval becomes [1,5]. Step 3: Process interval [6,9]: Check: 6 > 5 → no overlap and after, since inserted=False, add newInterval [1,5] to result, set inserted=True, then add [6,9] to result. Step 4: Result = [[1,5],[6,9]].

Step 1: Initialize result = [], inserted = False, newInterval = [4,8]. Step 2: Process [1,2]: 2 < 4 → no overlap, before, add to result: result=[[1,2]]. Step 3: Process [3,5]: Check overlap: 5 >= 4 and 3 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(4,3)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,5)=8, newInterval becomes [3,8]. Step 4: Process [6,7]: Check overlap: 7 >= 3 and 6 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(3,6)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,7)=8, newInterval stays [3,8]. Step 5: Process [8,10]: Check overlap: 8 >= 3 and 8 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(3,8)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,10)=10, newInterval becomes [3,10]. Step 6: Process [12,16]: Check: 12 > 10 → no overlap and after, since inserted=False, add newInterval [3,10] to result, set inserted=True, then add [12,16] to result. Step 7: Result = [[1,2],[3,10],[12,16]].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all intervals, manually check for overlaps with the new interval, and merge them by creating a new list. This involves repeatedly shifting or adjusting intervals in the list, which can be inefficient due to multiple passes or list modifications.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Insert Interval with Brute Force O(n²) Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution inserting a new interval into sorted non-overlapping intervals using inefficient brute force method.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach tackles the insert interval problem by treating it as a series of manual overlap checks and list reconstructions. The intuition is straightforward: we examine each existing interval, compare it with the new interval, and handle three scenarios—no overlap (keep as is), overlap (merge by expanding the new interval's bounds), or complete containment (skip the existing interval). This creates O(n²) complexity because after merging, we might need to re-check or shift subsequent intervals, especially when multiple overlaps occur. The algorithm starts by initializing an empty result list and a flag to track if the new interval has been inserted. We iterate through each interval in the input list. For each interval, we check if it overlaps with the new interval by comparing their start and end values. If they overlap, we merge them by updating the new interval's start to the minimum of both starts and end to the maximum of both ends. If the current interval ends before the new interval starts (no overlap and it should come before), we add it to the result. If the current interval starts after the new interval ends (no overlap and it should come after), we first add the merged new interval (if not already added) and then add the current interval. After the loop, if the new interval hasn't been added (e.g., it's at the end), we append it. The time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because merging might require re-scanning or adjusting the list multiple times, though typically it's O(n) for a single pass but the brute force nature implies potential quadratic behavior in implementations that rebuild the list repeatedly. Space complexity is O(n) for the result list. This connects to L1 by relying on the key subroutine of checking and merging overlaps. Common mistakes include incorrect overlap detection (e.g., using strict inequalities), forgetting to handle cases where the new interval is at the very beginning or end, and inefficient list manipulations that degrade performance. Real code pattern: Initialize result = [], inserted = False, for each interval in intervals: if interval.end < newInterval.start: result.append(interval), elif interval.start > newInterval.end: if not inserted: result.append(newInterval), inserted = True, result.append(interval), else: newInterval.start = min(newInterval.start, interval.start), newInterval.end = max(newInterval.end, interval.end), if not inserted: result.append(newInterval), return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = [], inserted = False, newInterval = [2,5]. Step 2: Process interval [1,3]: Check overlap: 3 >= 2 and 1 <= 5 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(2,1)=1, newInterval.end = max(5,3)=5, newInterval becomes [1,5]. Step 3: Process interval [6,9]: Check: 6 > 5 → no overlap and after, since inserted=False, add newInterval [1,5] to result, set inserted=True, then add [6,9] to result. Step 4: Result = [[1,5],[6,9]].

Step 1: Initialize result = [], inserted = False, newInterval = [4,8]. Step 2: Process [1,2]: 2 < 4 → no overlap, before, add to result: result=[[1,2]]. Step 3: Process [3,5]: Check overlap: 5 >= 4 and 3 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(4,3)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,5)=8, newInterval becomes [3,8]. Step 4: Process [6,7]: Check overlap: 7 >= 3 and 6 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(3,6)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,7)=8, newInterval stays [3,8]. Step 5: Process [8,10]: Check overlap: 8 >= 3 and 8 <= 8 → overlap, merge: newInterval.start = min(3,8)=3, newInterval.end = max(8,10)=10, newInterval becomes [3,10]. Step 6: Process [12,16]: Check: 12 > 10 → no overlap and after, since inserted=False, add newInterval [3,10] to result, set inserted=True, then add [12,16] to result. Step 7: Result = [[1,2],[3,10],[12,16]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Merge Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap and merge them into a single interval.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach, handling the core operation of detecting overlaps and combining intervals. The intuition is based on interval geometry: two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if they share any common points, which occurs when the end of one is greater than or equal to the start of the other and vice versa. Specifically, they overlap if max(a,c) ≤ min(b,d). Merging simplifies this by taking the minimum of the starts and maximum of the ends to create a new interval that covers both. This works because if intervals overlap, their union forms a contiguous range from the earliest start to the latest end. The algorithm involves two steps: first, check overlap by comparing if interval1.end >= interval2.start and interval2.end >= interval1.start. If true, merge by setting new start = min(interval1.start, interval2.start) and new end = max(interval1.end, interval2.end). If false, return the intervals separately. Time complexity is O(1) for constant-time comparisons, and space is O(1) for a few variables. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly in the main loop to handle each interval against the new interval. Common mistakes include using strict inequalities (e.g., > instead of >=) which misses touching intervals, incorrectly ordering the comparisons, or not updating the merged interval properly. Real code pattern: def overlap_and_merge(interval1, interval2): if interval1.end < interval2.start or interval2.end < interval1.start: return False, None else: new_start = min(interval1.start, interval2.start), new_end = max(interval1.end, interval2.end), return True, [new_start, new_end].

examples.trace: Step 1: Check overlap: interval1.end=3 >= interval2.start=2 → True, interval2.end=5 >= interval1.start=1 → True, so overlap exists. Step 2: Merge: new_start = min(1,2)=1, new_end = max(3,5)=5. Step 3: Return True and [1,5].
Step 1: Check overlap: interval1.end=3 >= interval2.start=4 → False (since 3 < 4), so no need to check second condition. Step 2: Return False and None.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Interval Comparison and List Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Process each interval sequentially, decide based on overlap with a target interval, and build a new list.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the iterative process that drives the brute force approach. The intuition is to traverse the sorted list of intervals once, making decisions at each step about whether to keep, merge, or insert intervals relative to a target (the new interval). This works because the intervals are sorted by start time, allowing a linear scan where we can determine positional relationships (before, overlapping, after) without backtracking. The algorithm maintains a result list and a flag for whether the target has been inserted. For each interval in the input: if it ends before the target starts, it belongs before and is added to result; if it starts after the target ends, it belongs after, so we first add the target (if not already) then the interval; if it overlaps, we merge it into the target by expanding the target's bounds. After the loop, if the target hasn't been added (e.g., it's at the end or all intervals were before), we append it. This yields O(n) time for the scan, but the brute force aspect comes from potential inefficiencies in list handling or multiple passes. Space is O(n) for the result. This connects to L1 by using the overlap check subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include incorrect condition orders (e.g., checking overlap before before/after cases), not updating the target properly during merges, or mishandling edge cases like empty lists. Real code pattern: result = [], inserted = False, for interval in intervals: if interval.end < target.start: result.append(interval), elif interval.start > target.end: if not inserted: result.append(target), inserted = True, result.append(interval), else: target.start = min(target.start, interval.start), target.end = max(target.end, interval.end), if not inserted: result.append(target), return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: result=[], inserted=False, target=[2,5]. Step 2: Interval [1,3]: 3 < 5? Check: 3 < 2? No, 3 >= 2, so check overlap: 3 >= 2 and 1 <= 5 → overlap, merge: target=[min(2,1)=1, max(5,3)=5]=[1,5]. Step 3: Interval [6,9]: 6 > 5 → after, since inserted=False, add target [1,5] to result, set inserted=True, add [6,9] to result. Step 4: result=[[1,5],[6,9]].

Step 1: result=[], inserted=False, target=[6,8]. Step 2: Interval [1,5]: 5 < 6 → before, add [1,5] to result. Step 3: After loop, inserted=False, add target [6,8] to result. Step 4: result=[[1,5],[6,8]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Updating Interval Boundaries
ladders.desc: Compare start and end values of intervals to determine order and update min/max for merging.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operations of comparing interval boundaries and updating them during merges, which is essential for the brute force logic. The intuition is that intervals are defined by their start and end points, and operations like checking order (before/after) or merging rely on simple numerical comparisons. By comparing starts and ends, we can decide if one interval is completely before another (end < start), after (start > end), or overlapping. Updating involves taking the minimum of starts and maximum of ends to expand an interval to cover a merged range. This works because intervals are contiguous ranges on a number line, and these comparisons capture all positional relationships. The algorithm involves: for two intervals A=[a,b] and B=[c,d], check if b < c (A before B), a > d (A after B), or else they overlap. If overlapping, compute new_start = min(a,c) and new_end = max(b,d). Time and space are O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the basic checks used in the iterative process. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors with comparisons (e.g., using <= instead of < for before/after), not handling equalities correctly, or confusing start and end order. Real code pattern: def compare_and_update(interval1, interval2): if interval1.end < interval2.start: return 'before', interval1, elif interval1.start > interval2.end: return 'after', interval1, else: new_start = min(interval1.start, interval2.start), new_end = max(interval1.end, interval2.end), return 'overlap', [new_start, new_end].

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare: interval1.end=3, interval2.start=4, 3 < 4 → True, so return 'before' and interval1 [1,3].

Step 1: Compare: interval1.end=5 < interval2.start=3? 5 < 3 → False. Step 2: interval1.start=1 > interval2.end=7? 1 > 7 → False. Step 3: Thus overlap, update: new_start = min(1,3)=1, new_end = max(5,7)=7, return 'overlap' and [1,7].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying List of Intervals
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental list operations such as iteration, appending, and updating elements on a list of intervals.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the essential list manipulations required in the brute force approach, focusing on how to handle a list where each element is an interval represented as a list or tuple of two numbers. The intuition is that lists are dynamic data structures allowing sequential access and modification, which is crucial for building the result list step by step. By iterating through the input list, we can examine each interval, make decisions, and construct a new list accordingly. This works because lists support operations like indexing, appending, and slicing in linear time. The algorithm involves: initialize an empty result list, loop through each interval in the input list using a for-loop, for each interval, perform checks (e.g., compare with new interval), and based on the outcome, append intervals to the result list or update variables. Time complexity for these operations is O(n) for iteration and O(1) average for appends, contributing to the overall O(n) scan. Space is O(n) for the new list. This connects to L3 by providing the structural framework to apply comparisons and updates. Common mistakes include modifying the input list while iterating (which can cause errors), incorrect indexing, or not initializing lists properly. Real code pattern: result = [], for i in range(len(intervals)): current = intervals[i], if condition: result.append(current), else: modify and append as needed, return result.

examples.input: intervals = [[1,3],[6,9]], operation: iterate and append non-overlapping intervals
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Iterate: i=0, current=[1,3], check: suppose no overlap, append to result: result=[[1,3]]. Step 3: i=1, current=[6,9], check: no overlap, append: result=[[1,3],[6,9]].

examples.input: intervals = [[1,5]], operation: update an interval during merge
examples.trace: Step 1: Suppose we have target=[2,3] and current=[1,5]. Step 2: Check overlap and merge: target.start = min(2,1)=1, target.end = max(3,5)=5, so target becomes [1,5]. Step 3: This involves modifying the target list in-place.

testCases.input: intervals = [[1,5]], operation: append interval to result
intervals = [[1,5],[6,10]], operation: iterate and collect all intervals
intervals = [[1,5],[6,10]], operation: append a new interval [11,15]
intervals = [[1,5],[6,10]], operation: check length of list
intervals = [[1,5],[6,10]], operation: iterate and skip first interval
intervals = [[1,5],[6,10]], operation: create a copy of list

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Intervals as Pairs of Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn what an interval is: a range defined by a start and end value, and basic properties like length and order.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the basic concept of intervals, which are fundamental to the problem. An interval is a contiguous range on a number line, represented as a pair of numbers [start, end] where start ≤ end. The intuition is that intervals model real-world ranges like time slots, distances, or numerical intervals, and understanding their properties is prerequisite for manipulating them. Key properties include: length = end - start, order based on start value (e.g., [1,3] comes before [4,5] because 1 < 4), and relationships like before, after, or overlapping. This works because intervals are simple mathematical objects that can be compared using basic arithmetic. The algorithm for this level is conceptual: define an interval, explain how to represent it as a list or tuple, and demonstrate basic comparisons like checking if start <= end. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. This connects to L4 by providing the basic data structure that lists will store. Common mistakes include representing intervals with start > end, confusing open vs. closed intervals, or not understanding that intervals include all points between start and end. Real code pattern: interval = [start, end], assert start <= end, print(f'Interval from {start} to {end}').

examples.trace: Step 1: Start = 2, end = 5. Step 2: Check: 2 ≤ 5 → True, so valid interval. Step 3: Represent as list [2,5].
Step 1: Interval A: [1,3], Interval B: [4,6]. Step 2: Check order: A.end = 3, B.start = 4, since 3 < 4, A is completely before B.

testCases.input: Check if [1,5] is a valid interval
Check if [5,1] is a valid interval

approaches.name: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the sorted intervals once, adding non-overlapping intervals to a result list and merging overlapping ones with the new interval on the fly. This uses the sorted order to efficiently handle overlaps in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Insert Interval with Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution to insert a new interval into sorted non-overlapping intervals using single-pass traversal.
ladders.explanation: The linear scan approach leverages the sorted nature of the input intervals to efficiently insert and merge a new interval in O(n) time. The intuition is that we can process intervals in order, handling three cases: intervals that come completely before the new interval (add directly), intervals that overlap with the new interval (merge continuously), and intervals that come completely after (add directly). By maintaining a 'current' merged interval that expands as we encounter overlaps, we ensure all overlapping intervals are combined into one. The algorithm initializes an empty result list and sets a flag to track whether the new interval has been inserted. We iterate through each existing interval: if it ends before the new interval starts, it's added to result; if it starts after the new interval ends, we first add the merged new interval (if not already added) then the current interval; otherwise, they overlap, so we update the new interval's start to min(starts) and end to max(ends). After the loop, if the new interval wasn't added, append it. This works because sorting guarantees we encounter intervals in increasing order, so once we pass the new interval's end, no further overlaps can occur. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space is O(n) for the output list. Common mistakes include not handling empty input lists, forgetting to merge multiple overlapping intervals, or incorrectly comparing interval boundaries. This directly implements the complete linear scan method described in the approach summary.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize result = [], inserted = False, new = [2,5].
2. Process [1,3]: Since 3 >= 2 (overlap), update new to [min(1,2), max(3,5)] = [1,5].
3. Process [6,9]: Since 6 > 5 (after), set inserted = True, add new [1,5] to result, then add [6,9].
4. Loop ends, inserted is True, so return result = [[1,5],[6,9]].

1. Initialize result = [], inserted = False, new = [4,8].
2. Process [1,2]: 2 < 4 (before), add to result.
3. Process [3,5]: 5 >= 4 (overlap), update new to [min(3,4), max(5,8)] = [3,8].
4. Process [6,7]: 7 >= 3 (overlap), update new to [min(3,6), max(8,7)] = [3,8].
5. Process [8,10]: 8 >= 3 (overlap), update new to [min(3,8), max(8,10)] = [3,10].
6. Process [12,16]: 12 > 10 (after), set inserted = True, add new [3,10] to result, then add [12,16].
7. Loop ends, inserted is True, return [[1,2],[3,10],[12,16]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Overlapping Intervals with a New Interval
ladders.desc: Function to merge a new interval with existing intervals that overlap, updating boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the linear scan approach, handling the merging logic when overlaps occur. The intuition is that when two intervals overlap, their union forms a single interval covering from the minimum start to the maximum end. Given a current new interval (which may already be merged from previous overlaps) and an existing interval, we check for overlap by comparing if the existing interval starts before or at the new interval's end, and ends at or after the new interval's start. If they overlap, we update the new interval's start to the minimum of both starts and the end to the maximum of both ends. This effectively expands the new interval to encompass all overlapping intervals. The algorithm takes two intervals as input and returns an updated new interval if they overlap, or indicates no overlap. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly during the scan to merge multiple intervals. Common mistakes include using strict inequalities incorrectly (e.g., checking only if starts are equal) or not updating both boundaries. The real code pattern involves simple min/max operations.

examples.trace:
1. Check overlap: interval start 1 <= new end 5 AND interval end 3 >= new start 2 → True.
2. Update new start = min(1,2) = 1.
3. Update new end = max(5,3) = 5.
4. Return [1,5].

1. Check overlap: 6 <= 8 AND 7 >= 3 → True.
2. Update new start = min(3,6) = 3 (unchanged).
3. Update new end = max(8,7) = 8 (unchanged).
4. Return [3,8].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Single-Pass Interval Insertion with Three Cases
ladders.desc: Algorithm to traverse sorted intervals once, categorizing each as before, overlapping, or after the new interval.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the linear scan strategy by processing each interval in order and handling three distinct cases based on comparison with the new interval. The intuition is that sorted intervals allow us to decide early: if an interval ends before the new interval starts, it belongs entirely before and can be added to the result immediately; if it starts after the new interval ends, it belongs entirely after, so we first add the merged new interval (if not already) then the interval; otherwise, they overlap, and we merge them into the new interval. This works because sorting ensures no interval before can overlap later, and once we pass the new interval's end, all subsequent intervals are after. The algorithm iterates through intervals, using conditionals for each case, and maintains a flag to track if the new interval has been added. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, space O(1) extra besides output. This is the central technique in L0, enabling efficient insertion. Common mistakes include incorrect order of cases (e.g., checking overlap before before-case) or missing the flag logic. The real code pattern involves a loop with if-elif-else branches.

examples.trace:
1. Start with result = [], inserted = False.
2. For [1,3]: end 3 < new start 2? No. start 1 > new end 5? No. So overlap case: merge new to [1,5].
3. For [6,9]: end 6 < 1? No. start 6 > 5? Yes, after case: set inserted=True, add new [1,5] to result, add [6,9].
4. End loop, inserted True, return result.

1. Result = [], inserted = False.
2. [1,2]: 2 < 4? Yes, before case: add [1,2].
3. [3,5]: 5 < 4? No. 3 > 8? No. Overlap: merge new to [3,8].
4. [6,7]: 7 < 3? No. 6 > 8? No. Overlap: merge new to [3,8].
5. [8,10]: 10 < 3? No. 8 > 8? No (8 == 8, so overlap). Overlap: merge new to [3,10].
6. [12,16]: 12 < 3? No. 12 > 10? Yes, after case: set inserted=True, add new [3,10], add [12,16].
7. Return result.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Intervals for Overlap
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap by comparing their start and end points.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for identifying when intervals need merging. Two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if they share any common point, which occurs when a <= d and c <= b. The intuition is that the first interval must start before or at the second's end, and the second must start before or at the first's end; if both conditions hold, they intersect. For example, [1,3] and [2,4] overlap because 1 <= 4 and 2 <= 3. This comparison is symmetric and handles edge cases like touching intervals (e.g., [1,2] and [2,3] overlap if we consider them mergeable, as in the problem). The algorithm simply checks these two inequalities. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L2 by being used in the overlap case. Common mistakes include using strict inequalities (e.g., a < d) which misses touching intervals, or checking only one condition. The real code pattern is a boolean expression.

examples.trace:
1. Check a <= d: 1 <= 4 → True.
2. Check c <= b: 2 <= 3 → True.
3. Both True, so overlap.

1. Check a <= d: 1 <= 5 → True.
2. Check c <= b: 4 <= 3 → False.
3. Not both True, so no overlap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Merging Two Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Combine two overlapping intervals into one by taking the minimum start and maximum end.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation creates a single interval that covers the union of two overlapping intervals. Given intervals [a,b] and [c,d] that overlap, the merged interval is [min(a,c), max(b,d)]. The intuition is that the union spans from the earliest start to the latest end, ensuring all points from both intervals are included. For example, merging [1,3] and [2,4] yields [1,4]. This operation assumes the intervals overlap; if they don't, merging isn't defined. The algorithm computes min and max of the boundaries. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L3 by being applied after overlap detection. Common mistakes include swapping min and max or not verifying overlap first. The real code pattern uses built-in min and max functions.

examples.trace:
1. Since they overlap, compute start = min(1,2) = 1.
2. Compute end = max(3,4) = 4.
3. Return [1,4].

1. Overlap confirmed, start = min(3,6) = 3.
2. End = max(8,7) = 8.
3. Return [3,8].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Representing and Comparing Intervals
ladders.desc: Understand intervals as pairs of numbers and compare them using basic inequalities.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces intervals as a basic data structure, represented by a start and end value (e.g., [1,3] means all numbers from 1 to 3 inclusive). The intuition is that intervals model continuous ranges, and we can compare them using simple numeric comparisons: an interval [a,b] comes before [c,d] if b < c, and after if a > d. For example, [1,3] is before [4,5] because 3 < 4. This is prerequisite for higher levels because sorting and overlap detection rely on these comparisons. The algorithm involves storing intervals as lists or tuples and using comparison operators on their endpoints. Time and space are O(1) per comparison. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic elements for merging. Common mistakes include confusing start and end or misapplying inequalities. The real code pattern uses indexing like interval[0] for start and interval[1] for end.

examples.trace:
1. Check end of first: 3.
2. Check start of second: 4.
3. Since 3 < 4, first is before second.

1. Check start of first: 6.
2. Check end of second: 5.
3. Since 6 > 5, first is after second.

approaches.name: Binary Search O(n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search to find the insertion point for the new interval, then merge overlapping intervals by scanning left and right from that point. This reduces the number of comparisons needed to locate overlaps compared to a full linear scan.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Insert Interval with Binary Search Merge
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search to find insertion point, then merge overlapping intervals by scanning left and right.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that since intervals are sorted and non-overlapping, we can use binary search to efficiently locate where the new interval should be inserted, then handle merging by scanning only the intervals that potentially overlap. This reduces unnecessary comparisons compared to a full linear scan. The algorithm works because binary search gives us O(log n) time to find the insertion index, and then we only need to examine intervals that could overlap with the new interval, which in worst case is O(n) when many intervals need merging. Step-by-step: 1) Use binary search to find the position where new interval's start would be inserted while maintaining sorted order. 2) Initialize result array and copy all intervals before insertion point. 3) Scan left from insertion point to merge any intervals that overlap with new interval (intervals ending at or after new interval's start). 4) Scan right from insertion point to merge any intervals that overlap with updated new interval (intervals starting at or before new interval's end). 5) Add the merged new interval to result. 6) Copy remaining non-overlapping intervals after the merged region. Time complexity is O(n) because in worst case we might need to merge all intervals. Space complexity is O(n) for the result array. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search insertion subroutine from L1 as a key component. Common mistakes include: not handling empty intervals list properly, incorrect binary search implementation that doesn't find the correct insertion point, and failing to merge intervals correctly when new interval overlaps multiple intervals on both sides. Real code pattern: def insert(intervals, newInterval): if not intervals: return [newInterval]; insert_idx = binary_search(intervals, newInterval.start); result = intervals[:insert_idx]; # Merge left while insert_idx > 0 and intervals[insert_idx-1].end >= newInterval.start: newInterval.start = min(newInterval.start, intervals[insert_idx-1].start); insert_idx -= 1; # Merge right i = insert_idx; while i < len(intervals) and intervals[i].start <= newInterval.end: newInterval.end = max(newInterval.end, intervals[i].end); i += 1; result.append(newInterval); result.extend(intervals[i:]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Binary search for insertion point of start=2. Compare with [1,3]: 2 >= 1, move right. End of search, insertion index = 1. Step 2: Copy intervals before index 1: result = [[1,3]]. Step 3: Merge left: Check interval at index 0 ([1,3]): end=3 >= new start=2 → merge. New interval becomes [min(1,2), max(3,5)] = [1,5]. Decrement insert_idx to 0. Step 4: Merge right: Start from i=0, check interval at i=0 ([1,3]): already merged, skip. i=1 ([6,9]): start=6 > new end=5 → stop merging. Step 5: Add new interval: result.append([1,5]). Step 6: Copy remaining: intervals[1:] = [[6,9]] → result = [[1,5],[6,9]].

Step 1: Binary search for insertion point of start=4. Compare with middle [6,7]: 4 < 6, move left. Compare with [3,5]: 4 >= 3, move right. Insertion index = 2. Step 2: Copy intervals before index 2: result = [[1,2],[3,5]]. Step 3: Merge left: Check interval at index 1 ([3,5]): end=5 >= new start=4 → merge. New interval becomes [min(3,4), max(5,8)] = [3,8]. Decrement insert_idx to 1. Check interval at index 0 ([1,2]): end=2 < new start=3 → stop left merge. Step 4: Merge right: Start from i=1, check interval at i=1 ([3,5]): already merged, skip. i=2 ([6,7]): start=6 <= new end=8 → merge. New interval becomes [3, max(8,7)] = [3,8]. i=3 ([8,10]): start=8 <= new end=8 → merge. New interval becomes [3, max(8,10)] = [3,10]. i=4 ([12,16]): start=12 > new end=10 → stop merging. Step 5: Add new interval: result.append([3,10]). Step 6: Copy remaining: intervals[4:] = [[12,16]] → result = [[1,2],[3,10],[12,16]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Insertion Point
ladders.desc: Find the index where a new interval's start should be inserted in sorted intervals using binary search.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that since intervals are sorted by start time, we can use binary search to efficiently locate the position where a new value (the new interval's start) would fit while maintaining sorted order. This works because binary search operates on sorted arrays by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half, giving us O(log n) time complexity. The algorithm: 1) Initialize left=0, right=len(intervals). 2) While left < right, calculate mid = (left+right)//2. 3) Compare new_start with intervals[mid].start. If new_start < intervals[mid].start, set right=mid (search left half). Else, set left=mid+1 (search right half). 4) When loop ends, left is the insertion index. Time complexity is O(log n), space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the insertion point used in the full solution to know where to begin merging. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop conditions, incorrect comparison logic that doesn't handle equal values properly, and not handling empty intervals list. Real code pattern: def binary_search_insert(intervals, new_start): left, right = 0, len(intervals); while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if new_start < intervals[mid][0]: right = mid; else: left = mid+1; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=2. Step 2: mid=(0+2)//2=1, intervals[1][0]=6, new_start=2 < 6 → right=1. Step 3: left=0, right=1, mid=(0+1)//2=0, intervals[0][0]=1, new_start=2 >= 1 → left=1. Step 4: left=1, right=1, loop ends. Return left=1.

Step 1: left=0, right=5. Step 2: mid=(0+5)//2=2, intervals[2][0]=6, new_start=4 < 6 → right=2. Step 3: left=0, right=2, mid=(0+2)//2=1, intervals[1][0]=3, new_start=4 >= 3 → left=2. Step 4: left=2, right=2, loop ends. Return left=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merge Overlapping Intervals from Insertion Point
ladders.desc: Scan left and right from an insertion point to merge intervals that overlap with a new interval.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that once we know where to insert the new interval, we only need to check intervals near that point for overlaps, rather than scanning the entire list. This works because intervals are sorted, so any interval that could overlap must be adjacent to the insertion point. The algorithm: Given intervals list, new interval [s,e], and insertion index idx. 1) Merge left: Starting from idx-1 and moving leftwards, while current interval's end >= s, update s = min(s, current interval's start) and decrement idx. 2) Merge right: Starting from idx and moving rightwards, while current interval's start <= e, update e = max(e, current interval's end) and increment counter. 3) The merged interval is [s,e]. Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of intervals merged, worst case O(n). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the insertion index from binary search as starting point. Common mistakes include: not updating the insertion index correctly during left merge, incorrect overlap conditions, and not handling edge cases like no overlaps. Real code pattern: def merge_from_point(intervals, newInterval, insert_idx): s, e = newInterval; i = insert_idx; while i > 0 and intervals[i-1][1] >= s: s = min(s, intervals[i-1][0]); i -= 1; j = i; while j < len(intervals) and intervals[j][0] <= e: e = max(e, intervals[j][1]); j += 1; return i, j, [s,e].

examples.trace: Step 1: s=2, e=5, i=1. Merge left: Check i-1=0, interval [1,3]: end=3 >= s=2 → update s=min(2,1)=1, i=0. Check i-1=-1 (invalid) → stop left merge. Step 2: j=i=0. Merge right: Check j=0, interval [1,3]: start=1 <= e=5 → update e=max(5,3)=5, j=1. Check j=1, interval [6,9]: start=6 > e=5 → stop right merge. Step 3: Return i=0, j=1, merged interval [1,5].

Step 1: s=4, e=8, i=2. Merge left: Check i-1=1, interval [3,5]: end=5 >= s=4 → update s=min(4,3)=3, i=1. Check i-1=0, interval [1,2]: end=2 < s=3 → stop left merge. Step 2: j=i=1. Merge right: Check j=1, interval [3,5]: start=3 <= e=8 → update e=max(8,5)=8, j=2. Check j=2, interval [6,7]: start=6 <= e=8 → update e=max(8,7)=8, j=3. Check j=3, interval [8,10]: start=8 <= e=8 → update e=max(8,10)=10, j=4. Check j=4, interval [12,16]: start=12 > e=10 → stop right merge. Step 3: Return i=1, j=4, merged interval [3,10].

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Interval Overlap
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap and compute their merged interval if they do.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that two intervals overlap if one starts before or at the same time as the other ends. This is fundamental because merging intervals requires identifying overlaps. The condition: intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if a <= d and c <= b. If they overlap, the merged interval is [min(a,c), max(b,d)]. This works because the merged interval must cover the entire range of both intervals. Step-by-step: 1) Check if a <= d and c <= b. 2) If true, compute merged start = min(a,c), merged end = max(b,d). 3) If false, intervals do not overlap. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L2: This is used repeatedly in the merge logic to check and merge individual intervals. Common mistakes include: using incorrect overlap conditions (e.g., only checking if one interval is completely within another), and not handling equal endpoints correctly. Real code pattern: def overlaps(interval1, interval2): a,b = interval1; c,d = interval2; if a <= d and c <= b: return True, [min(a,c), max(b,d)]; else: return False, None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check a=1 <= d=5 → True. Check c=2 <= b=3 → True. Step 2: Both conditions true, so overlap exists. Step 3: merged start = min(1,2)=1, merged end = max(3,5)=5. Return True, [1,5].
Step 1: Check a=1 <= d=5 → True. Check c=3 <= b=2 → False. Step 2: Second condition false, so no overlap. Return False, None.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update Interval Boundaries
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and update the start or end of an interval to expand its range.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that when merging intervals, we need to expand the new interval to cover all overlapping intervals by updating its start to the minimum and its end to the maximum of all involved intervals. This is a basic operation because it's used repeatedly during merging. The algorithm: Given current interval [s,e] and another interval [a,b], update s = min(s,a) and e = max(e,b). This works because the merged interval must include the entire range. Step-by-step: 1) Compare s and a, set s to the smaller value. 2) Compare e and b, set e to the larger value. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This is used within the overlap check to compute the merged interval. Common mistakes include: updating in wrong order, or not updating both start and end when necessary. Real code pattern: def update_interval(current, other): s,e = current; a,b = other; s = min(s,a); e = max(e,b); return [s,e].

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare s=2 and a=1, min is 1 → s=1. Step 2: Compare e=5 and b=3, max is 5 → e=5. Return [1,5].
Step 1: Compare s=3 and a=6, min is 3 → s=3. Step 2: Compare e=8 and b=10, max is 10 → e=10. Return [3,10].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Intervals and Sorting
ladders.desc: Learn what intervals are and how sorting by start time organizes them.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an interval represents a continuous range between two points (e.g., time slots), and sorting intervals by their start time makes it easier to process them in order. This is the foundation because the binary search approach relies on intervals being sorted. An interval is defined by a start and end value, where start <= end. Sorting a list of intervals by start means arranging them so that the first element has the smallest start, the next has the next smallest, etc. This works because it allows efficient searching and merging. Step-by-step: 1) Define an interval as [start, end]. 2) To sort, compare intervals based on start value; if starts are equal, compare ends. 3) Sorted intervals have non-decreasing start times. Time complexity for sorting is O(n log n) with algorithms like quicksort, but in this problem intervals are already sorted. Space is O(1) for in-place sort. Connection to L4: Sorted intervals enable the update operations to be applied in a predictable order. Common mistakes include: not understanding that intervals are inclusive of endpoints, or confusing start and end values. Real code pattern: intervals = [[1,3],[6,9],[2,5]]; sorted_intervals = sorted(intervals, key=lambda x: x[0]); # Result: [[1,3],[2,5],[6,9]].

examples.trace: Step 1: List intervals: [6,9], [1,3], [2,5]. Step 2: Extract start values: 6, 1, 2. Step 3: Sort by start: smallest is 1 → [1,3], next is 2 → [2,5], largest is 6 → [6,9]. Step 4: Resulting sorted list: [[1,3],[2,5],[6,9]].

Step 1: List intervals: [3,5], [1,2], [1,4]. Step 2: Extract start values: 3, 1, 1. Step 3: Sort by start: smallest is 1, but two intervals have start=1, so compare ends: 2 and 4, order [1,2] then [1,4], then [3,5]. Step 4: Resulting sorted list: [[1,2],[1,4],[3,5]].
